primary_diagnosis.name: Behcet's disease (mild mucocutaneous) — acute oral aphthous ulcer flare with early dehydration risk

pathophysiology_summary: Behcet's disease is a variable-vessel vasculitis whose mildest phenotype is recurrent oral (and sometimes genital) aphthous ulceration. A severe oral flare causes odynophagia that can limit fluid intake and precipitate dehydration — the acute risk here, amplified by living alone. The ulcers themselves are benign and self-limiting (heal in 7-10 days) and respond to topical anaesthetic and corticosteroid therapy. The clinical tasks are: (1) gauge dehydration severity remotely (urine output, postural symptoms, ability to swallow saliva), (2) exclude the mimics (herpetic gingivostomatitis, hand-foot-and-mouth) and (3) screen for Behcet's systemic involvement (ocular, genital, neurological) that would change urgency.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent small shallow aphthous ulcers without vesicles, worse than usual, no fever, in a patient with a longstanding history.
Can cause painful oral ulcers but usually with preceding vesicles, gingival involvement and fever.
12 hours without fluids and postural dizziness — must assess severity as the acute safety issue.
Screen for palm/sole lesions and fever; absent here.

Aphthous morphology without vesicles/fever (DI-003), no systemic Behcet's involvement (DI-002), with only early/mild dehydration (DI-001) that is still self-correctable with topical analgesia and a fluid trial.
Early/mild (some dark urine, postural symptoms, still swallowing saliva) — manage with analgesia-enabled oral rehydration and close safety-net, escalate if it worsens.
No preceding vesicles, gingivitis or fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: topical anaesthetic ± corticosteroid to enable a supervised oral-fluid trial, explicit dehydration safety-net (given lives alone), and Behcet's systemic-involvement safety-net. Escalate to same-day face-to-face if unable to rehydrate.

red_flags.description: Progressive dehydration — minimal/no urine, worsening postural dizziness, or inability to swallow saliva
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals the need for face-to-face assessment and possible IV rehydration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated, dehydration progresses to pre-syncope/collapse and acute kidney injury, especially living alone.

red_flags.description: Behcet's systemic involvement — genital ulcers, painful red eye/visual change, or neurological symptoms
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ocular or neuro-Behcet's is sight-/organ-threatening and needs urgent specialist care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed ocular Behcet's can cause permanent vision loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sending every 'can't drink a drop' oral-ulcer flare to ED for IV fluids without first gauging true hydration or trying analgesia-enabled oral rehydration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the dehydration or systemic red flags
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing only on the ulcers and failing to assess urine output/postural symptoms or to screen for ocular/genital/neuro Behcet's.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Objective hydration status (mucous membranes on exam, capillary refill, postural BP/HR)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Home topical analgesia and supervised oral-fluid trial with a strict dehydration safety-net; same-day face-to-face assessment if unable to maintain intake or postural symptoms worsen.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Under-estimating dehydration in someone living alone; or over-escalating a benign, self-correctable flare.
